coredata.dc:title: Specific colorimetric detection of methylmercury based on peroxidase-like activity regulation of carbon dots/Au NPs nanozyme

coredata.dc:description: Methylmercury (MeHg+) is one of the common organic species of mercury, and has much higher toxicity than inorganic mercury. Based on the selective enhancement of the activity of nanozyme (NA-CDs/AuNPs) by MeHg+, a novel colorimetric nanoprobe for MeHg+ assay is proposed. The noradrenaline-based carbon dots (NA-CDs) as the reducing agent was applied to prepare the NA-CDs/AuNPs. The formation of gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs) between nanozyme and MeHg+ allows to simultaneously accelerate the electron transfer from Au and Hg to NA-CDs and the generation of radicals (i.e. ∙OH, ∙O2 - and ∙CH3). The NA-CDs/AuNPs has an outstanding anti-interference performance even in the presence of different mercury. Further density functionality theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the formation of Au@HgNPs via MeHg+ contributes to the significantly lowered activation energy, resulting in the peroxidase-like activity generation and acceleration. This leads to rapid (10 min) and specific colorimetric detection of MeHg+ with the detection limit of 0.06 μg L−1. This introduces a novel method for simple and sensitive detection of MeHg+, giving a new horizon for the assay of organometallic compounds.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of NA-CDs/AuNPs (a); Colorimetric assay of MeHg+ by increasing the POD-like activity of Au@HgNPs amalgam (b); Selectivity of NA-CDs/AuNPs on MeHg+ and its mechanism (c).
The computationally optimized structure for Hg2+ and MeHg+ on the Au(111) surface (a); TEM images of the NA-CDs/AuNPs (b) and Au@HgNPs (c); XPS spectra of the NA-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs (d).
UV–vis spectra of NA-CDs, NA-CDs/AuNPs, NA-CDs/AuNPs+Hg2+ and +MeHg+ (a); POD-like activity of NA-CDs, NA-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs (b-d).

UV–vis spectra of TMB+H2O2 + [NA-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+] with different scavengers (IPA, TEMPOL and Fe(II)-EDTA) (a); UV–vis spectra of TMB+H2O2 + [NA-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+] system in the different amount of TBA and PBQ (b).

The 1H NMR of MeHg+, MeHg++NA-CDs, MeHg++AuNPs and MeHg++NA-CDs/AuNPs system (a); EPR spectra of the NA-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+ system under the different incubation time (b); EPR spectra for the detection of ∙CH3 (c), ∙O2 - (d) and ∙OH (e) in Au@HgNPs and AuNPs+MeHg+ system; Zeta potential of NA-CDs/AuNPs with the addition of MeHg+, HgCl2, and Hg(NO3)2 (f).
DFT-calculated potential energy profile of the POD-like activity of Au(111)@Hg (a) and Au(111) (b).

UV–vis spectra of NA-CDs/AuNPs+H2O2 +TMB system after the addition of other metal ions (a), and NA-CDs/AuNPs+H2O2 +TMB+MeHg+ system after the addition of inorganic ions (b); Effects of MeHg+ and inorganic Hg2+ on the NA-CDs/AuNPs+H2O2 +TMB system (c); Comparison of the time-dependent catalytic effects of NA-CDs/AuNPs on Hg2+ and MeHg+ (d).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methylmercury (MeHg+) is one of the common organic species of mercury, and has much higher toxicity than inorganic mercury. Based on the selective enhancement of the activity of nanozyme (NA-CDs/AuNPs) by MeHg+, a novel colorimetric nanoprobe for MeHg+ assay is proposed. The noradrenaline-based carbon dots (NA-CDs) as the reducing agent was applied to prepare the NA-CDs/AuNPs. The formation of gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs) between nanozyme and MeHg+ allows to simultaneously accelerate the electron transfer from Au and Hg to NA-CDs and the generation of radicals (i.e. ∙OH, ∙O2 - and ∙CH3). The NA-CDs/AuNPs has an outstanding anti-interference performance even in the presence of different mercury. Further density functionality theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the formation of Au@HgNPs via MeHg+ contributes to the significantly lowered activation energy, resulting in the peroxidase-like activity generation and acceleration. This leads to rapid (10 min) and specific colorimetric detection of MeHg+ with the detection limit of 0.06 μg L−1. This introduces a novel method for simple and sensitive detection of MeHg+, giving a new horizon for the assay of organometallic compounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Methylmercury (MeHg+), the most common organic species of mercury, is well-known to be highly toxic, carcinogenic and threaten human health seriously, even at extremely low concentration (Selin, 2018; Deng et al., 2015). In particular, MeHg+ has raised strong concern due to its high bioaccumulation in the muscle and biomagnification factor (up to 106) in the food chain, especially, in aquatic species (Khan et al., 2019; Lavoie et al., 2013; Poulain and Barkay, 2013). Hg2+ ions deposited under the action of bacteria are converted into MeHg+ (Poulain and Barkay, 2013). Biomagnified MeHg+ is eventually ingested into the body through the consumption of seafood, etc. and causes several serious human health problems, including sensory, neurological and motor (Albers et al., 2022). International authority of the World Health Organization (WHO) has established strict limits for MeHg+ concentrations in food (500–1000 μg kg−1) (Akiyama et al., 2022; Vieira et al., 2015). Thus, sensitive, specific, and fast detection methods for MeHg+ species is important for avoiding ecotoxicological risks and understanding the chemical cycle of mercury in the environment (Bergquist and Blum, 2007; Bjerregaard and Møller, 2021).

Liquid chromatography-atomic fluorescence spectrometry (LC-AFS) (Chen et al., 2014), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Chen et al., 2015a, 2015b), and etc. (Carrasco et al., 2009) have been widely employed to detect MeHg+ owing to their high sensitivity. These methods, although very sensitive, require chromatography to differentiate between MeHg+ and inorganic mercury ions. Gas chromatography (GC) and high-performance liquid chromatography (LC) were widely used to separate and identify the rather complex species of mercury, which are very costly, and time-consuming making normal assessment of MeHg+ hampered (Costas-Mora et al., 2014). Alternatively, colorimetric detection methods for MeHg+ have received attention due to convenience, simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and rapid visual detection without the aid of any other advanced facilities (Xie et al., 2018; Donati et al., 2019). Regarding the development of colorimetric sensor for MeHg+, derivative reaction (Deng et al., 2017), functionalized nanoparticles (Aulsebrook et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017), and aptamers (Chen et al., 2018) have been developed, showing great potential for detection of MeHg+. Unlike inorganic species, it is difficult to detect MeHg+ with portable nanosensors due to the lack of suitable reducing/oxidizing agents. The use of strong reducing/oxidizing agents may increase the reaction rate and conversion of MeHg+, but there are serious problems related to their interference with inorganic mercury ions.

Donati et al. (Donati et al., 2019) proposed a colorimetric readout based on AuNPs catalyze efficient MeHg+ reduction to the metallic form (Hg0) amalgam formation using formic acid as the weak reductant. However, this detection system is not stable and susceptible to interference, which limits its further application. Therefore, the development of a new approach that can alleviate these problems and, more importantly, allow the MeHg+ assay directly without interference from Hg2+ ions is highly desirable. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are one of the most used materials in nanotechnology with inherent multifunctionality both catalytic property and enzyme-like activities (Zandieh and Liu, 2021; Hafez et al., 2019). But enzyme-like AuNPs are limited by low activity and poor colloidal stability, resulting in application hindered and more efforts to improve enzyme-like activities (Han et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2020). Phenol-based preparation of carbon dots (CDs) has good reducing ability as green reducing agents for the synthesis of AuNPs (Feng et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022). Several groups have demonstrated that CDs/metal nanocomposites show enhanced catalytic activity (Ma et al., 2017; Zhuang et al., 2016).

In this work, such limitations have been overcome by using the carbon dots-based AuNPs. For the first time, CDs-based AuNPs (i.e. NA-CDs/AuNPs) with good reducibility can react with MeHg+ to form Au-Hg (Au@HgNPs) amalgam, which could greatly enhance the peroxidase (POD)-mimicking activity. The POD-mimicking activity increasing of Au NPs has been reported in present Hg2+, due to the formation of Au-Hg amalgam (Sui et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2018; Logan et al., 2021). While our research discovered that the rate of formation of Au-Hg amalgam by Hg2+ (30–60 min) is much lower than that by MeHg+ (5–6 min) due to the difference of activation energy between NA-CDs/AuNPs and Hg2+/MeHg+. The formed Au@HgNPs could oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, colorless) to an intense blue product oxTMB, so that MeHg+ can be measured with the naked-eye as fast as 10 min ( Fig. 1). Yet the innovation of this work was the skillful use of MeHg+ to modulate POD-like activity for a highly selective colorimetric assay. Further density functionality theory (DFT) calculations were also conducted to disclose the potential mechanism for this significant effect.

NA-CDs was synthesized by one-step microwave digestion system. Briefly, 27.6 mg of NA and 2.1 g of citric acid (CA) monohydrate were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized (DI) water, and then 50 μL of ethylenediamine (EDA) was added to the mixed solution to form a clear solution under ultrasound. The mixed solution was then transferred to a 60 mL Teflon microwave vessel, and the temperature of the reaction was risen to 180 °C in 1 min using 850 W microwave irradiations for 60 min. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (11,180 g) for 15 min and was dialyzed in a dialysis tubing (500 D). After 24 h, the solution was concentrated using a rotary evaporator, and then freeze-dried to obtain the NA-CDs powder.

Briefly, 35 μL of 0.2 mg mL−1 NA-CDs solution and 100 μL of 1% HAuCl4 solutions were added into 25 mL ultra-pure water and then stirred for 25 min at 90 ℃. Next, the obtained solution was immediately cooled in an ice bath. Finally, AuNPs powder was obtained by lyophilizing dialyzed solution.

In a 1.5 mL tube, 10 μL of NA-CDs/AuNPs solution was added to 990 μL of aqueous sample containing various concentrations (2.5–20 nM) of MeHg+. The mixture was then shaken and incubated for 10 min 3 mL 0.2 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing 50 mM H2O2, 150 μM TMB were added and incubated for 5 min. UV–vis spectra (400–800 nm) were acquired with small volume cuvette or flat transparent 96 multiwell plate.

The POD-like activity was evaluated thoroughly by typically chromogenic reactions using TMB as substrates with the presence of H2O2. A reaction mixture containing 100 μL of 100 mM H2O2, 1 mg mL−1 of NA-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs, and 100 μL of citrate buffer of pH 4.0 was prepared. The change in the absorbance values of the NA-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs was monitored at wavelength 652 nm in the UV−vis spectrometer. Kinetic experiments, the kinetic assays of Au@HgNPs with TMB as the substrate, were performed by adding different concentrations of H2O2 and TMB by immobilizing the concentration of TMB at 0.2 mM and H2O2 at 10 mM, respectively. The absorbances were measured (652 nm) at different reaction times (within 60 s). The kinetic parameters (K m and V max) were calculated from Lineweaver-Burk plot:

where v is the reaction rate, [S] is the concentration of the substrate (H2O2 or TMB), K m is the Michaelis constant, and V max is the maximal reaction rate.

DFT calculations were carried out to evaluate adsorption energy and activation energy by the Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) within the GGA-PBE functional (Perdew et al., 1996a, 1996b). A four-layered (4 ×4) unit cell Au(111) slab was selected to model the gold surface with MeHg+ treatment, and the bottom two layers of Au were frozen during the all calculations. The plane-wave cut-off energy of 400 eV was used and the 3 × 3 × 1 K-point mesh was set by the Monkhorst-Pack method. All systems have included the vacuum space of 15 Å. The convergence criteria of energy and forces were set as 10−6 and 0.02 eV Å−1 to optimize structures, respectively. The variation in adsorption energy for each procedure could be calculated with.
(2) ΔE = E Au@HgNPs+mol – (E Au@HgNPs + E mol)
Where ΔE represents the energy difference of adsorption, E mol represents the energy of adsorbates (e.g·H2O2,·OH and etc.), E Au@HgNPs represents the energy of Hg2+ adsorption on Au(111) surface, and the E Au@HgNPs+mol represents the total energy after the adsorption between the surface of Au@HgNPs and adsorbate.

In previous reports, we know that Hg2+ could obviously enhance the POD-like activity of AuNPs by forming Au@Hg amalgam (Long et al., 2011a, 2011b), which can promote the obvious oxidation and color change of TMB to achieve the selective and sensitive assay of Hg2+ (Zhang et al., 2015; Long et al., 2011a, 2011b). The formed Au@Hg nanocomposites were the key point to enhancing the POD-like activity of AuNPs, while MeHg+ could not be reduced to Hg0 to form Au@Hg amalgam. We performed DFT calculations to study the energy changes for the chemisorption of Hg2+ and MeHg+ molecules on an Au(111) surface. The results ( Fig. 2 a) revealed that the adsorption energy of MeHg+ is thermodynamically disfavored (1.82 eV) and their kinetics is extremely slow. In contrast, the adsorption energy of Hg2+ is energetically favorable with a reaction energy of − 0.38 eV (Fig. 2 a).

In this work, the weak and suitable reducing NA-CDs/AuNPs enable efficient and rapid reduction of MeHg+ to form Au@Hg amalgam on the interface, while the selective reduction of MeHg+ by NA-CDs/AuNPs allowed for colorimetric readout upon analyte detection without interference from Hg2+ (Fig. 1). In our assay, NA-CDs/AuNPs possesses weak POD-like activity, and can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. NA-CDs/AuNPs with both reduction and POD-like activity can catalyze the reduction of MeHg＋ to Hg0, resulting in the NA-CDs/AuNPs surface being covered by Au-Hg amalgam (Lin et al., 2010; Chen et al., 2014). The electrons transfer from Au@HgNPs are much easier than from Au due to the absolute electronegativity of Hg(2.0) smaller than that of Au(2.54). The results demonstrated that the generation of ∙OH and ∙O2 - radicals from O2 and ∙CH3 radicals from the methyl group in MeHg＋are responsible for the enhanced POD-like activity after the introduction of MeHg+, which is consistent with literature (Chen et al., 2015a, 2015b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication, characterization of NA-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the structure of NA on MeHg+ recognition, three structurally similar materials (Fig. S1), including adrenaline, dopamine and octamine were also used to prepare reducing carbon dots (i.e. AD-CDs, DA-CDs and OA-CDs). As shown in Fig. S2a, the absorbance of nanozyme+H2O2 +TMB and [nanozyme+MeHg+]+H2O2 +TMB systems suggests that the enhancement of POD-like activity of NA-CDs/AuNPs is the strongest under the same content of MeHg+, and MeHg+ could also increase the POD-like activity of OA-CDs to a certain extent, which is mainly due to the difference of the -OH group affecting electron transfer (Dimić et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the presence of -CH3 electron-donating group in adrenaline also hinders the transfer of electrons from MeHg+ and AuNPs to carbon dots (Dimić et al., 2017). The absence of -OH on aliphatic chains in adrenaline also affects intramolecular hydrogen bond formation and electron transfer (Dimić et al., 2020). Herein, the selected NA-CDs was used to prepare NA-CDs/AuNPs. The stability of the NA-CDs/AuNPs was analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and UV–vis, it was found (Fig. S2b) that carbon dots can significantly improve the stability of gold nanoparticles, which is similar to our previous study (Yang et al., 2022). Additionally, the reducibility of the as-prepared nanozyme was evaluated through the KMnO4 fading experiment. As shown in Fig. S2c, the reducibility follows the following order: NA-CDs/AuNPs > OA-CDs/AuNPs > AD-CDs/AuNPs > DA-CDs/AuNPs, which revealed that NA-CDs/AuNPs is more favorable for reducing MgHg+ to Hg0.

The morphologies of the NA-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs are shown in Fig. 2 b and c. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the NA-CDs/AuNPs show the particle size of 10–20 nm without obvious agglomeration (Fig. 2 b). Compared to NA-CDs/AuNPs, Au@HgNPs with average sizes of 70–80 nm are all aggregated, which revealed the presence of mercury amalgam (Fig. 2 c). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (inset of Fig. 2 b and c) clearly show the lattice fringes with 0.242 nm, which can be assigned to the (111) plane of Au. Energy-dispersive X-Ray spectrum (EDX) analysis further reveals the presence of Au, Hg, C, N and O atoms in the Au@HgNPs sample (Fig. S3), showing the homogeneous distribution of Hg on the surface of NA-CDs/AuNPs. In addition, the peaks at 3573 cm−1, 1408 cm−1, 1192 cm−1, 1073 cm−1and 632 cm−1 in Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of NA-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs indicate the presence of OH, C-H, C-OH and CC stretching vibration, respectively (Fig. S4), which confirmed the successful preparation of NA-CDs/AuNPs by NA-CDs reduction. These results were also conformed the reduction of MeHg+ to the metallic form (Hg0) to form Au@Hg amalgam.

To further conform the formation of mercury amalgam, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied for the investigation of NA-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs. As shown in Fig. 2 d, the four typical characteristic peaks at 286.1 eV, 401.1 eV, 533.1 eV and 88.1 eV are response for typical C, N, O and Au elements for NA-CDs/AuNPs, and the five typical characteristic peaks at 284.1 eV, 401.1 eV, 532.1 eV, 87.1 eV and 104.1 eV are response for typical C, N, O, Au and Hg elements for Au@HgNPs, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Au4f, C1s, N1s, O1s and Hg4f were fitted (Fig. S4). The XPS peaks of Au 4 f are characterized by two sets of doublets (Fig. S4a), and the signals located at 84.1 and 87.8 eV for NA-CDs/AuNPs and at 85.2 and 88.1 eV for Au@HgNPs correspond to the Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2 of metallic Au(0). After the formation of mercury amalgam, these is a shift with 1.1 and 0.3 eV to higher binding energies, indicating the increasing of electron loss (Greczynski and Hultman, 2020; Tauson et al., 2010). The change of the binding energy implies that MeHg＋ promotes much stronger catalytic reactions. Meanwhile, two high-intensities of Hg4f band were observed after the formation of Hg0 on the surface of NA-CDs/AuNPs (Fig. S5b) (Costas-Mora et al., 2014).

Similarly, the change of binding energy of C, N and O elements was also observed in Au@HgNPs after 10 min of adding MeHg+, but not for Hg2+. The C1s signal was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.5, 285.4, 286.9, and 288.7 eV for NA-CDs/AuNPs and at 284.4, 284.9 and 288.0 eV for Au@HgNPs, which corresponded to C−C, C−N, C−O, and CO, respectively (Fig. S5c and d). The N1s spectrum displays two characteristic signals with binding energy values of 398.1 and 400.4 eV for NA-CDs/AuNPs and 398.0 and 399.9 eV for Au@HgNPs, which are attributed to the pyrrolic N and quaternary N configurations, respectively (Fig. S5e and f). The O1s spectrum fits three peaks at 531.6, 532.5 and 533.5 eV for NA-CDs/AuNPs and 531.4 and 532.4 eV for Au@HgNPs, corresponding to the -CO, OC-O-, and -C-OH groups, respectively (Fig. S5g and h) (Chen et al., 2015a, 2015b). Compared with the binding energy of C, N and O in Au@HgNPs and NA-CDs/AuNPs, the binding energy after the formation of mercury amalgam moves to the lower energy position, indicating that electrons flow from Au and Hg to NA-CDs during the nanozyme-catalyzed reaction.

According to previous reports, we know that the form of mercury may have a significant effect on the enzymatic activity of AuNPs. So, HgCl2 and Hg(NO3)2 were selected to interact with NA-CDs/AuNPs. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a that only MeHg+ can give the color changes and plasmon shift changes in the solution containing NA-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+ within 5 min. It turned out that HgCl2 and Hg(NO3)2 could not be reduced by NA-CDs/AuNPs to Hg0 within 40 min (Fig. 7 d). These results demonstrated that there was no interference for the formation of Au@HgNPs in the presence of Hg2+. More interestingly, the results in Fig. 7 d also revealed that NA-CDs/AuNPs did not show the reduction of Hg2+ until 40 min later, which is consistent with the results of DLS analysis for size measurement (Fig. S6). The selective reduction of MeHg+ by NA-CDs/AuNPs provides the possibility to construct a specific detection of MeHg+ based on changes in enzyme activity.

The POD-like activity was evaluated by employing TMB, (2, 2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the substrates. In Fig. 3 b-c, NA-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs catalyze TMB and ABTS with H2O2 to form the corresponding chromogenic products oxTMB (blue) and oxABTS (green) with the significant absorption peaks at 652 and 416 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, NA-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs can also catalyze OPD with H2O2 to produce the yellow fluorescent product (DAP) at 448 nm excitation wavelength ( Fig. 3 d). The Au@HgNPs possessed higher intrinsic POD-like activity than NA-CDs/AuNPs. To furtherly assess the catalytic efficiency of Au@HgNPs, the typical Michaelis−Menten curves were measured (Fig. S7). The detailed kinetics parameters are listed in Table S1. Using H2O2 and TMB as substrates, the enzyme kinetic constants (K m) of Au@HgNPs toward TMB and H2O2 were 0.255 mM and 7.847 mM, respectively, which were both clearly lower than that of HRP (1.383 and 7.921 mM), suggesting that Au@HgNPs exhibited a higher affinity toward the TMB substrate than that of HRP. Furthermore, the K m value of Au@HgNPs was much lower than that of NA-CDs/AuNPs, which indicated that the presence of Hg is conducive to enhance the substrate affinity.

The mechanism of nanoparticle enzyme-like activity is dependent of the reduction of H2O2 to produce reactive oxygen radical species such as ∙OH, ∙O2 -/∙OOH, ∙CH3. The generation of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) was investigated by trapping experiments during the catalytic reaction with 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPOL) as ∙O2 -/∙OOH scavengers, isopropanol (IPA) as ∙OH scavengers, Fe(II)-EDTA as H2O2 scavengers, respectively (Ning et al., 2017; Mvango and Mashazi, 2019). As shown in Fig. 4 a, when IPA was added into the TMB+H2O2 + [NA-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+] system, the absorbance intensity had the maximum change, indicating that ∙OH was the main reactive species. The addition of TEMPOL can scavenge the ∙O2 -/∙OOH in catalytic process, and the results showed that it has a slight inhibitory effect on the catalytic activity. The addition of Fe(II)-EDTA as H2O2 scavengers also showed an obviously effect, revealing that the POD-like activity of NA-CDs/AuNPs played a major role in the reaction system. Meanwhile, different amount of tert-butanol (TBA) as ∙OH scavenger and p-benzoquinone (PBQ) as ∙O2 - scavenger were also added into the reaction system (Inset of Fig. 4 b). The results (Fig. 4 b) conformed the significant role of ∙OH during the oxidation process of TMB. To further identify the ∙OH produced from NA-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs, p-hydroxybenzoic acid (PTA) was applied to obtain information on the ∙OH and active radicals, respectively (Xing et al., 2015). After addition of PTA capturing ∙OH (generating fluorescent signal at 440 nm), the Au@HgNPs displays a stronger fluorescent signal than that of NA-CDs/AuNPs (Fig. S8a). The ascorbic acid (AA) was always employed for the detection of total ROS. Seen from Fig. S8b, after the addition of AA (scavenger of ROS) into the NA-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs system, the absorbance of AA in Au@HgNPs system was significantly lower than that of NA-CDs/AuNPs system. These results no doubt confirmed that the formation of mercury amalgam plays a key role in the production of ∙OH and ROS.

To verify this discovery, potential mechanisms were further investigated by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), zeta potential and DFT. Fig. 5 a shows the 1H NMR spectrum of 100 μL MeHg+ (10 μg L−1) added to 100 μL 60% D2O, 100 μL NA-CDs, 100 μL AuNPs (prepared by citric acid) and 100 μL NA-CDs/AuNPs, respectively, which is characterized by a sharp signal of its -CH3 group around 0.86 ppm. As can be seen, the addition of NA-CDs and citrate-capped AuNPs did not make any detectable change in the spectrum, as such NA-CDs or citrate-capped AuNPs alone is not able to reduce MeHg＋ (Fig. 5 a). In particular, in presence of the NA-CDs/AuNPs, an obvious descent in the signal of MeHg+ was observable after incubation few minutes, revealing suitable catalytic activity of NA-CDs/AuNPs for MeHg+. Noteworthy, in the spectrum of MeHg+NA-CDs/AuNPs system, the 1H NMR signal of the methane produced during the reaction was also seen (Donati et al., 2019), while no methane signals could be observed in the spectra acquired without citrate-capped AuNPs or NA-CDs. Therefore, the NA-CDs/AuNPs is beneficial for the reducing reaction of MeHg＋ to metallic (Hg0) to form gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs).

We used electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technology to detect ROS in the reaction system. As shown in Fig. 5 b, the EPR spectra demonstrates that ∙OH, ∙O2 - and ∙CH3 are involved in the NA-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+ system. The enhanced signal of ∙OH, ∙O2 - and ∙CH3 are also observed in the system after the formation of Au@HgNPs. It is noticed that the peak intensities of ∙OH, ∙O2 - and ∙CH3 in the Au@HgNPs system (Fig. 5 c-e) are higher than that in citrate-capped AuNPs/MeHg+ system, demonstrating the higher concentrations of ROS is generated by MeHg+ to induce an improved POD-like activity (Deng et al., 2015). In addition, the signal of ∙CH3 is also seen in the system which comes from a secondary reaction of the DMSO with the H2O2 or the free radical ∙OH, suggesting that more ROS were involved in the catalytic reaction to promote the POD-like activity.

The zeta potential measurement shows the potential of − 26.2 mV, − 20.9 mV, − 25.7 mV and 3.75 mV (Fig. 5 f) for NA-CDs/AuNPs, NA-CDs/AuNPs+HgCl2, NA-CDs/AuNPs+Hg(NO3)2 and NA-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+, respectively. The results suggested that the negative charges on the surface of NA-CDs/AuNPs can easily combine with positively charged MeHg+ to form Au@HgNPs. Conversely, the zeta potentials were slightly changed when HgCl2 and Hg(NO3)2 were added into the NA-CDs/AuNPs system, which is conducive to the selective detection of MeHg+.

Last, we fulfilled DFT calculations to check the mechanisms responsible for the PODs-like activity enhancement. The calculation results showed that the POD-like activity of Au(111) and Au(111)@Hg can be carried out via the following reactions (Chen et al., 2021; Li et al., 2015):.
(3) H2O2 = 2∙OH
(4) 2∙OH = H2O∙ + O

As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, The adsorption energies of H2O2 on Au(111) and Au(111)@Hg surfaces are − 0.17 eV (see structure a1) and − 0.114 eV (see structure a2), respectively. The results revealed that H2O2 is more easily adsorbed near the Hg atom, and its adsorption energy is more thermodynamically favorable. Hg atoms could significantly increase the affinity between the Au(111) surface and H2O2. The cleavage of the O-O bond of H2O2 (i.e. a1-b1-c1) is an important step that determines the reaction rate. The reaction energy barriers of Au(111)@Hg and Au(111) are 0.33 eV and 0.45 eV, respectively, thus, H2O2 is more easily adsorbed and activated on Au(111)@Hg than on pure Au(111), which is in accordance with the observed stronger peroxidase-like activity of Au(111)@Hg is consistent with the results.

Based on our discovering, we developed a novel strategy for the assay of MeHg＋, which relies on the NA-CDs/AuNPs to reduce MeHg+ to form gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs) with high the POD-like activity, and that the Au@HgNPs could highly efficiently catalyze oxidizing TMB to give an intense blue product. The factors that affected the effect of this catalysis include pH, NaCl concentration, nanozyme (NA-CDs/AuNPs), temperature, and the concentration of substrates (H2O2, TMB) and were systematically optimized. Fig. S9 shows that the optimal pH, NaCl concentration, NA-CDs/AuNPs concentration, temperature and the concentration of substrates (H2O2, TMB) were 7, < 10 mg mL−1, 12.5 μg mL−1, 40 ℃, 1.5 mM and 150 μM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of MeHg＋
ce:section.ce:para: The strategy is based on MeHg+ stimulated POD-like property, TMB was oxidized to blue ox-TMB and the absorbance value at 652 nm increases with the increasing amount of MeHg＋ (Fig. S10a). A linear plot was derived from the collected absorbance values against the MeHg+ concentration (Fig. S10b) in the range of 0.375–75 µg L−1 (R2 = 0.9937). The detection limit was 0.06 μg L−1 based on the calculation as of the signal to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3, which is much lower than the maximum allowable level of MeHg in meat fish food at 1.0 mg kg−1 defined by the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) (Li et al., 2018). Besides, comparison with other methods is summarized in Table S2, our method is superior to many other detection methods.

To verify the selectivity of the proposed method, different ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Cl−, CO3 2-, HCO3 2-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, NO3 -, NO2 -, 50-fold higher than the LOD) and Hg2+(same concentration with MeHg＋) were added to aqueous solutions containing NA-CDs/AuNPs, H2O2 and TMB. The results ( Fig. 7 a and b) showed only MeHg＋ can change the UV absorption characteristics of NA-CDs/AuNPs+H2O2 +TMB system (i. e. the generation of new absorption peaks at 687 nm) within few minutes (Fig. 7 c). As shown in Fig. S11, the colorimetric analysis of MeHg+ was interfered by Hg2+ within 10 min only when the concentration of Hg2+ was more than 10 times the concentration of MeHg+. At the same concentration, the effect of Hg2+ is slower than that of MeHg＋, and it will change around 45 min, while MeHg＋ has formed a relatively stable Au-Hg amalgamation around 10 min (Fig. 7 d). As compared to other ions, the addition of MeHg+ to the reaction solution showed obvious absorption peaks at 652 nm. Meanwhile, only the addition of MeHg+ could result in an obvious color change from colorless to intense blue. Consequently, the Au@HgNPs colorimetric sense can specifically recognize MeHg＋ in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MeHg+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Determination of MeHg+ in aquatic products using the proposed colorimetric method was performed after spiking the fish and shrimp samples with standard MeHg+ concentration. As shown in Table S3, the concentration of MeHg+ was found to be 27 ± 3 μg kg−1 in shrimp sample extract. The spiking (5, 30, 60 µg kg−1) recoveries for MeHg+ were ranged in 101.19–116.12%. These results demonstrated that the established method could be applied to the analysis of MeHg+ in real samples.

In summary, for the first time, a colorimetric assay for MeHg+ determination was proposed based on the highly regulation of MeHg+ on the POD-like activity of NA-CDs/AuNPs. We found the formed Au@HgNPs was the key point to enhancing the POD-like activity of NA-CDs/AuNPs. Unlike the other Au@Hg nanocomposites formed by Hg2+, the rate of formation of Au@Hg amalgam by MeHg+ is much higher than Hg2+ due to the presence of NA-CDs, which is expected to give a real-time and highly selective identification of MeHg+ in real applications. As revealed by the further DFT calculations, the lowered activation energy of Au@HgNPs was responsible for the acceleration of POD-like activity. This work introduces a novel strategy for MeHg+ assay, giving further insights to the design of nanozymes and the sensing of other organometallic in the future.

Methylmercury (MeHg+), the most common organic source of mercury, is much more toxic than inorganic mercury even at very low concentrations, and is mainly transformed from inorganic mercury by microorganisms. The interference of coexisting Hg2+ is the key point hindering the MeHg+ assay. In this study, the enzymatic activity regulation of the designed gold-based nanozymes by methylmercury was revealed and used to solve the interference problem of coexisting Hg2+, which also provides a novel strategy for other organometallics assay.
